body:
The United Arab Emirates does not have a single national digital-asset regulator. It has four bodies that each hold a defined geographic patch of UAE territory as 'the' virtual-asset regulator for that patch, plus a fifth, federal body that cuts across all four patches on two specific subjects rather than holding a patch of its own -- and understanding that layered map, before reading a single pillar board or trajectory entry on this site, is the single most important thing to carry into every page that follows for this jurisdiction. VARA -- the Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority, established under Dubai Law No. (4) of 2022 -- regulates virtual assets in the Emirate of Dubai, including Dubai's own free zones, but expressly excluding the Dubai International Financial Centre (DIFC). The Capital Market Authority (CMA) -- the UAE's federal capital-markets regulator, and, since 1 January 2026, the direct legal successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) -- regulates virtual assets everywhere else in the UAE outside the two financial free zones, currently under CMA Decision No. (4/R.M) of 2026, the CMA's Virtual Assets Framework. The Dubai Financial Services Authority (DFSA) regulates only the DIFC: a common-law financial free zone carved out of Dubai's own territory, and legally separate from both VARA's and the CMA's remits even though it sits inside Dubai geographically. The Financial Services Regulatory Authority (FSRA) regulates only the Abu Dhabi Global Market (ADGM), Abu Dhabi's own common-law financial free zone. Those four -- VARA, the CMA, the DFSA, and the FSRA -- are the jurisdiction's four geographic regulators, and every virtual-asset licensing rule on this site traces to exactly one of them. The fifth body, the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE), does not hold a geographic patch of its own; instead it cuts across all four of those regimes on two specific subjects: it runs the federal Payment Token Services Regulation governing stablecoins and payment tokens, and it holds banking licensing, prudential rules, and the Digital Dirham central-bank-digital-currency programme -- all regardless of which emirate a bank sits in -- while its own payment-token licensing regime still expressly carves the DIFC and ADGM back out, leaving stablecoin activity inside those two zones to the DFSA and FSRA respectively.

This layering is not an accident of drafting; it follows directly from how the UAE's federal legal system itself is built. Federal law -- Federal Law No. (8) of 2004 Regarding Financial Free Zones, as amended -- permits designated 'financial free zones' to run their own civil and commercial law, including their own common-law courts, distinct from the civil-law federal system that otherwise applies across the UAE; so far, only two zones have been designated this way, the DIFC (inside Dubai) and ADGM (inside Abu Dhabi). A necessary consequence of that legal carve-out is that each financial free zone needs its own financial regulator rather than falling under the federal regulator's supervision -- hence the DFSA for the DIFC and the FSRA for ADGM, each running its own rulebook, its own licensing regime, and its own enforcement, independent of federal oversight for the matters within its zone. Dubai then made a further, separate choice of its own: rather than leaving virtual-asset regulation to the federal regulator (as the rest of the UAE does) or folding it into the DFSA's DIFC-only remit, the Dubai government created VARA in 2022 as a dedicated, emirate-level virtual-asset authority covering Dubai's mainland and its non-financial free zones -- a deliberate policy choice, not a legal necessity, reflecting how central the virtual-asset sector already was to Dubai's own economic strategy by that point. No comparable dedicated virtual-asset authority exists at the federal level or in any other emirate; everywhere in the UAE outside Dubai's VARA-covered territory and the two financial free zones, virtual-asset activity falls to the CMA as part of its general federal capital-markets remit.

The practical rule that follows from all of this, and that this site enforces on every card it publishes, is simple and has no exceptions worth naming: a rule issued by VARA, the CMA, the DFSA, or the FSRA never applies outside that body's own geographic slice unless a separate federal law says so in terms. A VARA licence does not authorise activity in the DIFC or ADGM, or anywhere in the UAE outside Dubai's non-DIFC territory. The CMA's federal framework expressly excludes the DIFC and ADGM by its own statutory text. DFSA authorisation is good for the DIFC and nowhere else; FSRA authorisation is good for ADGM and nowhere else. The one deliberate exception -- discussed in its own section below -- is the UAE's federal anti-money-laundering and counter-terrorist-financing regime, which was built from the outset to reach across all five bodies at once. Because a claim that is true under VARA's rules can be flatly false under the CMA's, or under the DFSA's, or under the FSRA's, this site tags every factual claim with the specific regime it comes from -- naming the regulator and the geography -- rather than ever describing a rule as a general 'UAE rule.' Where a pillar board states a fact without a named regime attached, that should be read as a gap to flag, not as an implicit claim that a rule applies everywhere.

Who regulates what, in more detail

VARA licenses and supervises Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) conducting any of a defined set of VA Activities in the Emirate of Dubai outside the DIFC -- exchange services, broker-dealer services, custody services, advisory services, management and investment services, lending and borrowing services, VA transfer and settlement services, and virtual-asset issuance -- each governed by its own dedicated VARA rulebook, all sitting under the umbrella Virtual Assets and Related Activities Regulations 2023. The CMA licenses and supervises virtual-asset activity everywhere else in the UAE outside the DIFC and ADGM, under a framework that expanded from three licensed virtual-asset activities under the old SCA regime to eight under the CMA's 2026 Virtual Assets Framework, built across five modules covering general requirements, conduct of business, alternative trading systems, anti-money-laundering, and prudential standards. The DFSA regulates DIFC firms' Crypto Token business by extending its existing financial-service-activity categories -- dealing, arranging, advising, managing, and custody -- to cover Crypto Tokens, rather than building a wholly separate DIFC licence category from scratch. The FSRA takes a similar extend-the-existing-categories approach inside ADGM, and separately deems tokens exhibiting the characteristics of a Security to be 'Digital Securities' regulated under its ordinary securities framework. The CBUAE, finally, licenses Payment Token Issuers, Payment Token Custodians and Transferors, and Payment Token Conversion Providers under its Payment Token Services Regulation, and separately runs the UAE's bank licensing and prudential regime and its own Digital Dirham CBDC programme -- both applying UAE-wide, with the payment-token licensing specifically excluding the Financial Free Zones. Two further federal bodies matter without licensing anyone directly: the UAE Financial Intelligence Unit (the 'Unit', which operates the goAML suspicious-transaction-reporting platform) and the Executive Office for Control and Non-Proliferation (EOCN, responsible for implementing targeted financial sanctions) -- both defined directly in the federal AML/CFT statute discussed below, and both federal, cross-cutting institutions rather than any one regulator's creature.

The SCA-to-CMA succession

Older primary documents that this site's document library indexes -- and that this site's own summary cards sometimes still quote verbatim, where the source text has not itself been updated -- will refer throughout to the 'SCA', the Securities and Commodities Authority. That is not a sixth regulator, and it is not a body this site tracks separately from the CMA. Federal Decree-Law No. (32) of 2025 Concerning the Capital Market Authority, in effect from 1 January 2026, repealed the SCA's founding statute (Federal Law No. 4 of 2000) in its entirety and reconstituted the SCA as the Capital Market Authority (CMA), an independent federal public authority that succeeds the SCA 'in all its rights, obligations, and contracts.' Every reference to 'SCA' in a pre-2026 document, and in some documents published since that date whose own text has not caught up with the rename -- this site's research has directly observed the CBUAE's own live Payment Token Services Regulation still naming 'SCA' in a provision written before the succession took effect -- should be read as referring to the same body now called the CMA, not to a different or additional regulator. Where this site's cards quote 'SCA' from a source published before 1 January 2026, that is a historically accurate quotation of the source as it was written, not an error.

What is already in force, regime by regime

VARA's regime has been live the longest of the four Dubai-and-federal virtual-asset-specific frameworks: the Virtual Assets and Related Activities Regulations 2023 were first published in February 2023, and VARA's full suite of activity-specific rulebooks -- exchange, broker-dealer, custody, advisory, management and investment, and issuance, including the fiat-referenced-token issuance rules -- has been in force, in a current consolidated form, since 19 June 2025. The DFSA's Crypto Token regime has been in force since 1 November 2022, making it the DIFC's own longest-standing dedicated crypto framework; an updated version came into force on 12 January 2026, replacing a DFSA-maintained list of 'Recognised' tokens with firm-led, documented self-assessment against DFSA suitability criteria for most tokens, while keeping the DFSA itself as the direct gatekeeper specifically for Fiat Crypto Tokens. The FSRA's framework is the oldest of all four: it launched in June 2018 as the Gulf region's first dedicated crypto-asset regulatory regime, and was substantially amended, with immediate effect, on 10 June 2025 -- moving from FSRA pre-approval to firm self-assessment for which virtual assets a licensed firm may use, while formally prohibiting privacy tokens and algorithmic stablecoins outright. The CBUAE's Payment Token Services Regulation has been in force since 31 August 2024, and directly prohibits, UAE-wide outside the Financial Free Zones, both unlicensed payment-token activity and the issuance of algorithmic stablecoins or privacy tokens -- a prohibition its own text states binds even entities separately licensed by the SCA/CMA or a Local Licensing Authority such as VARA. The CMA's framework is the newest piece of this picture by a wide margin: Federal Decree-Laws No. (32) and No. (33) of 2025, which reconstituted the SCA as the CMA and recodified the federal capital-markets regime to expressly capture virtual assets, took effect only on 1 January 2026, and the CMA's own Virtual Assets Framework was announced on the CMA's website on 13 April 2026. That framework's own transition mechanism is still running as this page is written: Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025's Article (83) gives every person subject to the Decree-Law one calendar year from its 1 January 2026 commencement -- so, absent an extension the Council may grant at its discretion, until 1 January 2027 -- to bring their status into compliance.

The one federal exception: anti-money-laundering

Against this backdrop of geographically fragmented licensing, anti-money-laundering and counter-terrorist-and-proliferation-financing regulation stands out as the one pillar genuinely built to reach across all five bodies and all four geographies, including inside the DIFC and ADGM. Federal Decree by Law No. (10) of 2025, in force since 14 October 2025, defines the 'Supervisory Authority' for AML/CFT purposes as 'the federal and local authorities entrusted under the legislation with the supervision of the financial institutions, designated non-financial businesses and professions, virtual asset service providers, and non-profit organizations' -- language broad enough to make VARA, the CMA, the DFSA and the FSRA each the Supervisory Authority for the licensees within their own slice, on top of their own separate licensing rules. Its implementing regulation, Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025, has been in force since 14 December 2025. And a standalone CBUAE document, the United Arab Emirates' Virtual Assets Travel Rule, states in its own text that it applies to Virtual Asset Service Providers 'within the territorial scope of the UAE Federal and Emirate Laws, any Free Zone and any Financial Free Zone' -- meaning that specific document, alone among the instruments this site tracks for the UAE, genuinely reaches every VASP in the country regardless of which of the five bodies licenses it.

What is coming next

The clearest near-term date on this site's trajectory board is the CMA's own one-year transition deadline of 1 January 2027, running from Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025's 1 January 2026 commencement, by which entities and individuals subject to the Decree-Law must bring their status into compliance, subject to any discretionary extension the Council may grant. Multiple secondary legal-commentary sources report a more granular, phased implementation timeline specifically for the CMA's Virtual Assets Framework -- variously describing a pilot phase around mid-2026, a compliance deadline for larger firms around January 2027, and later deadlines for smaller firms and government entities running into late 2027 -- but this site has not yet been able to independently confirm those specific phased dates against the CMA's own primary decision text, so they are treated on this site's pillar boards as reported, not as confirmed fact. VARA's own AML/CFT supervisory assessment of VASPs' alignment with the new federal Executive Regulations is stated, in VARA's own March 2026 circular, to run 'during 2026,' with roughly half of that window remaining as this page is written. On the CBUAE side, the Digital Dirham programme's own July 2025 policy paper commits only to a staged rollout of retail, wholesale and cross-border versions 'in the coming years,' without a specific launch date in that primary source, while the CBUAE's cross-border 'Jisr' CBDC platform with China, launched in November 2025, is stated to expand further in 2026 with the addition of more participating central banks.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas for the UAE in one place, always broken out by which of the five regimes each claim belongs to. The trajectory board lists officially announced upcoming dates and windows -- including the CMA's transition deadline and VARA's 2026 assessment window above -- together in one place. The document library indexes every primary-source document the watcher has observed, tagged by regulator. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, including terms specific to this jurisdiction's layered structure, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source, and the specific regime, it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
